content.text: is the hook that reads a context value from the nearest matching Provider above the calling component in the tree. It replaces the legacy

render prop API with a single function call that returns the value directly. Understanding what it returns, when it triggers re-renders, and how to use it safely is the key to effective context consumption.

attrs.alt: useContext reads value from nearest Provider, skipping intermediate components

content.text: . The hook finds the nearest Provider above the component and returns its current
prop. No props or wiring are needed in any intermediate components.
The context object must be the same reference that was passed to the Provider. If you create two different context objects with
returns the default value, not the Provider's value.
The context object you pass to
must be the exact same object exported by the file that called
makes the component a subscriber. Whenever the Provider's
prop changes, all components that called
with that context will re-render — even if the change is in a part of the value they do not use.
This is the primary reason to split contexts by concern: if
holds both the user object and a UI preference flag, a component that reads only the flag will re-render whenever the user changes. Separate contexts mean separate subscriptions.
When there is no matching Provider above the component,
returns the default value passed to
default and a custom hook that checks for it, missing Providers produce a clear error.
When the context value is an object, destructure directly from the
return value. The component still subscribes to the whole context object — destructuring does not narrow the subscription.
multiple times with different context objects. Each call is an independent subscription.
The pre-hooks API used a render prop pattern:
replaces this entirely for function components. The Consumer API still works but adds nesting without benefit.
Rules of Hooks Apply to useContext
must be called at the top level of a function component — it cannot be called inside conditionals, loops, or nested functions. This is a React rules-of-hooks constraint, not specific to context.

React does not provide a built-in context selector. Every consumer re-renders when the context value changes, even if the specific fields it reads are unchanged. For high-frequency context updates (mouse position, scroll), consider splitting into separate contexts or using a state management library with selector support.
Props or external state library with selectors
Unit testing a component that calls
requires wrapping it in the appropriate Provider. Without the Provider,
— which may cause the component to throw or render incorrectly.
A test utility wrapper function reduces boilerplate when many tests need the same Provider setup.
React DevTools (browser extension) shows context values in the component tree. Select any component that calls
and the "Hooks" panel displays the current context value alongside the hook name. Setting
on the context object makes it identifiable.
, there are no stale closure issues with
. The hook always returns the current value from the Provider. Each render, the component reads the latest value directly — no dependency array, no risk of capturing an old value.
An effect that closes over a context value would capture it at effect-creation time. Read context inside the component (via
) and pass the result to the effect as a dependency if you need the current value inside effects.

Context is often introduced to avoid prop drilling — passing the same prop through many layers. However, not all deep prop chains are better served by context. The right tool depends on reusability and coupling.
2–3 levels deep; component is reusable in other trees
3+ levels deep; value is app-wide or tree-wide
Parent renders children and passes elements directly — no context needed
Context is compatible with lazy-loaded components. A component loaded with
and rendered inside a Provider will receive the context value normally once it mounts.
skips re-renders when props are unchanged, but context changes bypass the memo check entirely — the component always re-renders when context changes.

The correct strategy when you need to prevent re-renders from context is to pull the context read higher in the tree and pass the value as a prop to the memoised child. Now the child re-renders only when the prop changes, and the memo comparison applies.
When an effect needs to read a context value, list it as a dependency. Changes to the context value will trigger the effect to re-run.
were omitted from the dep array, the effect would run once on mount with the initial user value and never re-run, even if the user logs out and logs back in.
A few patterns that look correct but cause issues:
useContext returns default value; no error
Import from the file that called createContext
React hooks rule violation; runtime error
Move to top level of component
Expecting memo to block context updates
Lift context read and pass as prop
Forgetting to list context dep in useEffect
Effect uses stale value after context changes
Add context value to effect dep array
New context object each render; consumers see only default
subscribes the component to the nearest matching Provider and returns its current value. It re-renders the component whenever that value changes.
the Provider's value prop, or the createContext default if no Provider is found.
any change to the Provider's value reference — subscribed to the whole object, not individual fields.
destructuring the return value does not narrow the subscription.
call useContext multiple times in one component for different contexts — each is independent.
call at top level only; no conditionals or loops.
split contexts by update frequency for performance; React context has no built-in granular subscription.
